sentences.base_language: Jisu bought her KTX ticket weeks in advance, excited about visiting her parents in Busan.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 부산에 계신 부모님을 만날 생각에 설레며, 몇 주 전에 미리 KTX 티켓을 샀다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun Busan-e gyesin bumonim-eul mannal saenggak-e seollemyeo, myeot ju jeon-e miri KTX tiket-eul satda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 생각에** = at the thought of doing, in anticipation of: 만날 **생각에** 설레다 (be excited at the thought of meeting). **생각** (thought) + **에** (at/upon): this pattern expresses an emotional reaction triggered by anticipating a future event. Compare: **-(으)ㄹ 생각이다** (to intend to do), **-(으)ㄹ 생각에** (emotional response to the prospect of doing).
**계시다** = honorific form of 있다 (to be/exist): 부산에 **계신** 부모님 (parents who are in Busan — honorific). **계시다** is used when the subject is someone deserving respect: 선생님이 교실에 **계세요** (the teacher is in the classroom). The modifier form **계신** applies the same honorific respect in a relative clause.

sentences.base_language: On the morning of departure, she arrived at Seoul Station with a large suitcase and mixed feelings of excitement and nostalgia.
sentences.target_language: 출발 당일 아침, 지수는 커다란 여행 가방을 들고 설렘과 향수가 뒤섞인 복잡한 마음으로 서울역에 도착했다.
sentences.romanized: Chulbal dangilr achim, Jisu-neun keodaran yeohaeng kabang-eul deulgo seolleom-gwa hyangsu-ga dwisoekkin bokjaphan maeum-euro Seoul-yeok-e dochaghaeotda.
literal: on the morning of the departure day,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ** past modifier on action verbs: 설렘과 향수가 뒤섞**인** 마음 (feelings in which excitement and nostalgia were mixed). **뒤섞이다** (to be mixed together) + **-(으)ㄴ** creates a relative clause modifying 마음: the state of being mixed has already occurred. Compare the present modifier: 뒤섞이**는** 마음 (feelings that are mixing — in progress).
**-(으)로** marking manner/means: 복잡한 마음**으로** 도착하다 (arrive with complicated feelings). **-(으)로** attaches to nouns to indicate the manner, instrument, or state accompanying an action: 웃음**으로** 대답하다 (reply with a smile), 두 손**으로** 잡다 (hold with both hands). It differs from the subject/object markers and adds a 'by means of / in the state of' nuance.

sentences.base_language: The platform was crowded with all kinds of travelers hurrying to board their carriages.
sentences.target_language: 승강장은 각자의 칸에 타려고 서두르는 온갖 종류의 여행객들로 붐볐다.
sentences.romanized: Seunggjangjang-eun gakja-eui kan-e taryeogo seodureoneun onjat jongryu-eui yeohaengjaek-deul-ro bumbyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)려고** = in order to, with the intention of: 타**려고** 서두르다 (hurry in order to board). **-(으)려고** expresses purpose or intent preceding an action: 먹**으려고** 샀다 (bought in order to eat), 보**려고** 왔다 (came in order to see). It requires the same subject for both clauses. Compare: **-기 위해(서)** (in order to — more formal/written): 성공**하기 위해** 노력하다 (make an effort in order to succeed).
**-들로 붐비다** = to be crowded with [people]: 여행객**들로** 붐볐다 (was crowded with travelers). **붐비다** is an intransitive verb meaning 'to be crowded/bustling,' and the **-(으)로** particle marks the filling agent: 사람**들로** 가득하다 (full of people), 관광객**들로** 북적이다 (bustling with tourists). **붐비다** is more colloquial and lively than **혼잡하다** (to be congested — formal).

sentences.base_language: She found her reserved window seat and settled in with a novel and some snacks from home.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 예약한 창가 좌석을 찾아 자리를 잡고, 소설책과 집에서 가져온 간식을 꺼내 놓았다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun yeyakhan changga jwaseok-eul chaja jari-reul jabgo, soseolchaek-gwa jib-eseo gajyeon gan-sik-eul kkeonaejjohatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 놓다** = to do and leave in a resulting state: 꺼내 **놓았다** (took out and set [them] there). **-아/어 놓다** emphasizes that the result of an action is left in place for later use or reference: 미리 준비해 **놓다** (prepare and have ready in advance), 써 **놓다** (write down and keep). Compare: **-아/어 두다** (nearly identical — do and set aside for the future): the two are largely interchangeable, but **놓다** can suggest a more physical placing.
**자리를 잡다** = to take one's seat, to settle in: **자리** (place/seat) + **잡다** (to grab/take hold of). This collocation means more than sitting — it implies actively claiming and settling into a spot: 좋은 자리를 **잡다** (grab a good spot). Compare: **앉다** (to sit — the act of sitting), **자리를 잡다** (to settle in — getting established in a spot).

sentences.base_language: As the KTX left Seoul, the dense city gradually gave way to open countryside and mountains.
sentences.target_language: KTX가 서울을 벗어나자, 빽빽한 도시 풍경이 서서히 탁 트인 들판과 산으로 바뀌었다.
sentences.romanized: KTX-ga Seoul-eul beoseonaja, ppaekppaekan dosi punggyeong-i seoseohi tak teurin deulpan-gwa san-euro bakkwyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** = as soon as, upon: 벗어나**자** (upon leaving/as soon as it left). **-자** attaches directly to a verb stem and signals that the second action immediately followed: 문이 열리**자** 손님이 들어왔다 (as soon as the door opened, the guest came in). It implies a near-instantaneous sequence and is more literary than **-자마자** (the very moment — even more emphatic).
**탁 트이다** = to be wide open, to open up expansively: **탁 트인** 들판 (wide-open fields). **탁** is an adverb conveying sudden openness, and **트이다** (to open up/clear) describes a view or space becoming unobstructed: 시야가 **탁 트이다** (the view opens up). Compare: **탁 트인** (wide and open — scenic), **탁 트인 느낌** (a feeling of spaciousness/liberation).

sentences.base_language: Jisu felt a wave of calm come over her — something about train travel always had that effect on her.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 잔잔한 평온함이 밀려오는 것을 느꼈다. 기차 여행은 언제나 그녀에게 그런 효과를 가져다 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun janjjanhan pyeong-onham-i millyeoneun geot-eul neukkkyeotda. Gicha yeohaeng-eun eonjena geunyeo-ege geureon hyogwa-reul gajeoda jueotda.
literal: the thing that is washing over (nominalized)-OBJ
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 오다** directional: 밀려**오다** (to wash over, to come rolling in). **밀리다** (to be pushed/washed) + **오다** (to come toward): the compound describes movement directed toward the subject's location or inner state. Compare: **밀려가다** (to be swept away — from the speaker). **-아/어 오다** consistently marks motion or development coming toward the experiencer: 달려**오다** (run toward), 다가**오다** (approach).
**가져다 주다** = to bring (for someone), to deliver as a benefit: 효과를 **가져다 주다** (bring an effect). **가져다** (carrying over) + **주다** (give/benefit): a compound expressing delivery of something to another person's benefit. Compare: **가져오다** (to bring — neutral, just movement), **가져다 주다** (to bring and give — emphasizes the benefit to the recipient): 책을 **가져다 주었다** (brought the book for her).

sentences.base_language: After about an hour, the woman sitting next to her started a friendly conversation.
sentences.target_language: 한 시간쯤 지났을 때, 옆에 앉아 있던 여성이 먼저 다정하게 말을 걸어 왔다.
sentences.romanized: Han sigan-jjeum jinaesseul ttae, yeopee anja itdeon yeoseong-i meonjeo dajeonghaге mal-eul georeo watda.
literal: when about an hour had passed,
was sitting next to (past habitual modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었을 때** = when [something had happened]: 지났**을 때** (when [an hour] had passed). **-(으)ㄹ 때** normally uses the plain stem (갈 때 — when going), but **-았/었을 때** marks a past-completed point in time: 졸업했**을 때** (when I had graduated). This distinguishes an already-completed event from an ongoing one.
**말을 걸다** = to strike up a conversation, to speak to first: 다정하게 **말을 걸어 왔다** (came and spoke to her in a friendly way). **말을 걸다** implies initiating contact, not just speaking: 먼저 **말을 걸다** (speak to someone first). The addition of **-아/어 오다** (**걸어 왔다**) adds a directional nuance — the conversational overture came toward Jisu, highlighting that it originated with the other woman.

sentences.base_language: She turned out to be a retired teacher who took this same train every month to see her grandchildren.
sentences.target_language: 그 여성은 매달 이 기차를 타고 손자 손녀를 만나러 가는 퇴직 교사였다.
sentences.romanized: Geu yeoseong-eun maedal i gicha-reul tago sonja sonnyeo-reul mannaro ganeun toejik gyosa-yeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)러 가다** = to go in order to do: 만나**러 가다** (go to meet). **-(으)러** expresses the purpose of movement verbs like 가다, 오다, 다니다: 밥 먹**으러** 가다 (go to eat), 공부하**러** 오다 (come to study). It is exclusively used with movement verbs, unlike **-(으)려고** which can combine with any verb.
**-게 되다** becoming / resultant state: the sentence structure here implies she *turned out to be* (밝혀지다/알고 보니 ~였다), rendered naturally as ~**였다** with the narrative context supplying the 'turned out' meaning. Compare the explicit **-게 되다** pattern: 알게 **되다** (come to know/find out): 알고 보니 퇴직 교사**였다** (when we found out, she turned out to be a retired teacher) vs 퇴직 교사**가 되었다** (she became a retired teacher — a change of state).

sentences.base_language: Jisu's stomach growled and she realized she hadn't eaten anything since early morning.
sentences.target_language: 지수의 배에서 꼬르륵 소리가 나더니, 아침 일찍부터 아무것도 먹지 않았다는 것을 그제야 깨달았다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-eui bae-eseo kkoreureuk sori-ga nadeoeni, achim iljjikbuteo amugeotdo meokji anhatdaneun geot-eul geujеya kkaedareotda.
literal: a growling sound came and then,
the fact that she hadn't eaten (nominalized)-OBJ
sentences.grammarNote:
**-더니** = and then (after noticing), following a prior observation: 소리가 나**더니** 깨달았다 (a sound came, and then she realized). **-더니** connects two clauses where the speaker first perceived something and then the second event followed: 비가 오**더니** 무지개가 떴다 (it rained and then a rainbow appeared). It often marks a shift or consequence noticed by the speaker/subject.
**그제야** = only then, not until that moment: **그제야** 깨달았다 (realized only at that point). **그제야** implies that something should have been noticed earlier but wasn't until that specific moment — a mild sense of belated realization. Compare: **그때야** (at that moment — neutral timing), **그제야** (only then — implies delay or oversight): 말을 듣고 **그제야** 이해했다 (only after hearing it did I finally understand).

sentences.base_language: She went to the snack cart and bought a triangle kimbap and a can of barley tea.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 매점 카트로 가서 삼각 김밥 하나와 보리차 캔 하나를 샀다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun maejeom kateu-ro gaseo samgak gimbap hana-wa borcha kaen hana-reul satda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어서** cause/sequence: 가**서** 샀다 (went and bought). When **-아/어서** follows a movement verb, it expresses sequential actions — first going, then buying — rather than a cause-and-effect relationship. This sequential use is extremely common: 앉아**서** 먹다 (sit and eat), 찾아**서** 읽다 (find and read). The two actions are closely linked as a natural sequence.
**하나** as a counter/quantity word: 삼각 김밥 **하나**, 보리차 캔 **하나** (one triangle kimbap, one can of barley tea). In Korean, **하나** (one) follows the noun directly when used informally: 물 **하나** 주세요 (please give me one water). More formally, the appropriate counter word is used: **한 캔** (one can), **한 개** (one item). The informal pattern with bare **하나** is natural in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: The simple food tasted better than expected, perhaps because of the scenery flying past outside.
sentences.target_language: 아마도 창밖으로 빠르게 스쳐 지나가는 풍경 덕분인지, 소박한 음식이 예상보다 훨씬 맛있었다.
sentences.romanized: Amado changbak-euro ppareuge seuchyeo jinananeun punggyeong deokbuniji, sobaakhan eumsig-i yesangboda hwolssin masissotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**덕분인지** = perhaps thanks to, possibly because of: 풍경 **덕분인지** 맛있었다 (it was delicious, perhaps thanks to the scenery). **덕분** (thanks to/owing to — positive cause) + **인지** (whether/perhaps — expressing uncertainty). **-(으)ㄴ지/인지** marks an embedded question or uncertain reason: 왜인지 (for some reason), 무엇 때문인지 (perhaps because of something). The full phrase **덕분인지** hedges the causal claim.
**스쳐 지나가다** = to fly past, to pass by in a flash: 빠르게 **스쳐 지나가는** 풍경 (scenery flying past quickly). **스치다** (to graze/brush past) + **지나가다** (to pass by): a compound conveying quick, fleeting passage. Compare: **지나가다** (to pass by — neutral speed), **스쳐 지나가다** (to brush past — fleeting, barely noticed). Used figuratively too: 기억이 **스쳐 지나가다** (a memory flashes by).

sentences.base_language: Back at her seat, she dozed off for a bit and woke up just as the train passed through a long tunnel.
sentences.target_language: 자리로 돌아온 지수는 잠깐 居眠이 들었다가 기차가 긴 터널을 통과하는 순간 눈을 떴다.
sentences.romanized: Jari-ro doraon Jisu-neun jamkkan joereomiseo deureotdaga gicha-ga gin teonel-eul tonggwahaneun sungan nun-eul tteotda.
literal: the moment it was passing through
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었다가** = did and then (reversal or shift): 졸음이 들**었다가** 눈을 떴다 (dozed off and then woke up). **-았/었다가** indicates that a completed action was interrupted or reversed by the next action: 앉았**다가** 일어섰다 (sat down and then stood up), 잠들**었다가** 깼다 (fell asleep and then woke). The **-다가** element marks the interruption or change of state.
**-(으)는 순간** = the moment/instant when: 통과하**는 순간** 눈을 떴다 (opened her eyes the very moment it passed through). **순간** (瞬間, instant) with the present modifier **-는** captures an action at the precise instant of occurrence. Compare: **-(으)ㄹ 때** (when — more general time), **-(으)는 순간** (the very instant — emphasizing immediacy and simultaneity).

sentences.base_language: The train attendant came by to check tickets and announced quietly that the next stop would be in twenty minutes.
sentences.target_language: 승무원이 표를 확인하러 지나가다가, 다음 정거장까지 이십 분이 남았다고 조용히 알렸다.
sentences.romanized: Seungmuwon-i pyo-reul hwagin-haro jinanagadaga, daeum jeonggeojeong-kkaji isip bun-i namatdago joyonghi allyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다가** interruption: 지나가**다가** 알렸다 (while passing by, announced). **-다가** attaches to a verb stem to show that an ongoing action was interrupted by or led into another action: 걷**다가** 넘어졌다 (was walking and then fell), 먹**다가** 멈췄다 (was eating and then stopped). The first action was in progress when the second occurred, unlike **-아/어서** which shows clean sequential completion.
**-(으)러 지나가다** = to pass by in order to do: 표를 확인하**러** 지나가다 (pass by to check tickets). The **-(으)러** purpose marker combines with the movement verb **지나가다** (to pass by/through), describing the attendant's movement as purposeful. This is a common pattern in narrative to describe service roles moving through a space: 주문받**으러** 다가오다 (approach to take an order).

sentences.base_language: Jisu's back ached from sitting so long and she stood up to stretch her legs in the aisle.
sentences.target_language: 오래 앉아 있었더니 허리가 아파서, 지수는 자리에서 일어나 통로에서 다리를 쭉 폈다.
sentences.romanized: Orae anja isseotdeoni heori-ga apaseo, Jisu-neun jari-eseo irona tongro-eseo dari-reul jjuk pyeotda.
literal: because she had been sitting for a long time
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었더니** = because [I/one] had done (first-person cause with experiential result): 오래 앉아 있**었더니** 허리가 아프다 (my back hurts because I had been sitting for a long time). **-더니** in the first-person context expresses that the speaker performed an action and noticed a resulting state: 많이 먹**었더니** 배가 부르다 (I'm full because I ate a lot). This differs from the third-person **-더니** which marks an observed change.
**쭉 펴다** = to stretch out fully, to extend: 다리를 **쭉** 펴다 (stretch legs out). **쭉** is an adverb conveying extension in a straight line: **쭉** 뻗다 (stretch straight out), **쭉** 늘어서다 (stand in a long line). Combined with **펴다** (to stretch/open), it emphasizes full extension. Compare: **살짝 펴다** (stretch slightly), **쭉 펴다** (stretch all the way out).

sentences.base_language: A small child in the seat ahead was pointing excitedly at cows through the window and laughing.
sentences.target_language: 앞좌석의 어린아이가 창밖의 소들을 신나게 손가락으로 가리키며 깔깔 웃고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Ap jwaseok-eui eorin-ai-ga changbak-eui sodeul-eul sinnage sonkarak-euro garikimeyo kkalkkal utgo isseotda.
literal: the small child in the front seat-SUBJECT
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 있다** progressive: 깔깔 웃**고 있었다** (was laughing). **-고 있다** marks an action in progress: 먹**고 있다** (is eating), 달리**고 있다** (is running). The past progressive **-고 있었다** describes an ongoing action at a specific past moment: 그때 자**고 있었다** (was sleeping at that time). This is one of the most fundamental tense-aspect structures in Korean.
**신나다/신나게** = to be excited, thrilling: **신나게** 가리키며 (pointing excitedly). **신나다** (exciting/exhilarating — an adjective-verb) describes a state of being thrilled or energized: **신나는** 음악 (exciting music), **신나게** 놀다 (play to one's heart's content). The **-게** adverb form describes how an action is done. Compare: **들뜨다** (to be excited — slightly anxious/restless): 들떠서 잠을 못 잤다 (was too excited to sleep).

sentences.base_language: Jisu smiled and thought about her own childhood trips on this line with her family.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 미소를 지으며 이 노선에서 가족과 함께했던 어린 시절 여행들을 떠올렸다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun miso-reul jeumeyo i noseon-eseo gajok-gwa hamkke haetdeon eorin sijeol yeohaeng-deul-eul tteoollye otda.
literal: had done together with family (past habitual modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었던** habitual/past modifier: 가족과 함께했**던** 여행들 (trips she used to take with her family). **-았/었던** modifies a noun to describe a past state or repeated past action that is no longer ongoing: 자주 가**던** 카페 (the café I used to go to often), 함께했**던** 추억 (memories of times spent together). This differs from **-(으)ㄴ** which simply marks a completed action in the past.
**떠올리다** = to bring to mind, to recall: 여행들을 **떠올렸다** (recalled the trips). **떠오르다** (to float up, to come to mind — intransitive) vs **떠올리다** (to bring to mind — transitive/causative): 기억이 **떠올랐다** (a memory floated up), 기억을 **떠올렸다** (she recalled the memory). **떠** (floating) + **올리다** (to raise/bring up): the image is of a memory rising to the surface of consciousness.

sentences.base_language: As the train began to slow on approach to Busan Station, she started gathering her things with growing excitement.
sentences.target_language: 기차가 부산역에 다가오면서 속도를 줄이기 시작하자, 지수는 점점 커지는 설렘으로 짐을 챙기기 시작했다.
sentences.romanized: Gicha-ga Busan-yeok-e dagaoмyeonseo sokdo-reul juligi sijakaja, Jisu-neun jeomjeom keojineun seolleom-euro jim-eul chaengigi sijakhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous action: 다가오**면서** 속도를 줄이다 (slow down while approaching). **-면서** connects two actions happening at the same time by the same subject: 음악을 들으**면서** 달렸다 (ran while listening to music). Here it shows the train doing two things simultaneously — approaching and decelerating. Compare: **-며** (slightly more literary form of **-면서**).
**-기 시작하다** = to begin to do, to start doing: 속도를 줄이**기 시작하다** (begin to slow down), 짐을 챙기**기 시작하다** (begin to gather things). **-기** nominalizes the verb and **시작하다** (to begin) takes it as its object. This construction marks the onset of an action and is one of the most productive patterns in Korean for describing beginnings: 비가 오**기 시작했다** (it started raining).

sentences.base_language: She could already picture her mother and younger sister standing on the platform waiting for her.
sentences.target_language: 지수는 이미 어머니와 여동생이 승강장에 서서 자신을 기다리고 있을 모습을 눈앞에 그려 볼 수 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Jisu-neun imi eomeoni-wa yeodongsaeng-i seunggjangjang-e seoseo jasin-eul gidarigo isseul moseub-eul nun-ap-e geuryeo bol su isseotda.
literal: would be waiting for her (future modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 수 있다/없다** = can/cannot, to be able to: 그려 볼 **수 있었다** (was able to picture). **-(으)ㄹ 수 있다** expresses capability or possibility: 할 **수 있다** (can do), 먹을 **수 있다** (can eat). The past form **수 있었다** describes a capability that existed at a specific past moment. Compare: **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다** (cannot — complete impossibility), **-기 어렵다** (hard to do — difficulty rather than impossibility).
**-(으)ㄹ 모습** = the image/scene of [someone] doing: 기다리고 있**을** 모습 (the image of them waiting). **모습** (appearance/scene/image) + **-(으)ㄹ** future modifier: a very common pattern for imagining a future or hypothetical scene: 행복하게 웃을 **모습** (the image of smiling happily), 뛰어오**를 모습** (the sight of running toward [me]). It is warmer and more visual than abstract future statements.

sentences.base_language: The train arrived exactly on time and Jisu stepped off feeling stiff but very happy.
sentences.target_language: 기차는 정시에 정확히 도착했고, 지수는 몸이 뻐근했지만 매우 행복한 마음으로 내렸다.
sentences.romanized: Gicha-neun jeongsi-e jeonghwakhi dochaghaeotgo, Jisu-neun mom-i ppeogeunhaetjiman maeу haengbokan maeum-euro naelyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-지만** concession = but, however: 뻐근했**지만** 행복했다 (was stiff but happy). **-지만** connects two clauses in contrast or concession: 비싸**지만** 맛있다 (expensive but delicious), 힘들**지만** 보람 있다 (hard but rewarding). Unlike **-는데** (which can introduce background information), **-지만** is specifically contrastive and can appear in both spoken and written registers.
**뻐근하다** = to be stiff, achy (from overuse or prolonged position): 몸이 **뻐근하다** (the body is stiff/achy). **뻐근하다** describes the dull ache of muscles that have been in one position too long or worked hard: 어깨가 **뻐근하다** (shoulders are stiff), 온몸이 **뻐근하다** (the whole body aches). Compare: **쑤시다** (to throb/ache — sharper pain), **결리다** (to have a cramp/stitch): 옆구리가 **결린다** (I have a stitch in my side).

sentences.base_language: Walking toward the exit with her suitcase, she decided that no matter how fast planes were, nothing could replace the joy of traveling by train.
sentences.target_language: 여행 가방을 끌며 출구 쪽으로 걸어가면서, 지수는 비행기가 아무리 빠르더라도 기차 여행의 즐거움은 그 무엇으로도 대체할 수 없다고 마음속으로 결론지었다.
sentences.romanized: Yeohaeng kabang-eul kkeulmyeo chulgu jjok-euro georeogamyeonseo, Jisu-neun bihaenggi-ga amuri ppareudeorado gicha yeohaeng-eui jeulgeoum-eun geu mueos-euro-do daechehal su eopda-go maeumsogeuro gyeollon jieotda.
literal: no matter how fast planes might be
sentences.grammarNote:
**아무리 -더라도** = no matter how ~: 비행기가 아무리 빠르**더라도** (no matter how fast planes are). **아무리** (no matter how) + **-(으)더라도** (even if/even though — concessive): this combination creates an emphatic concession: 아무리 어렵**더라도** 포기하지 않겠다 (I won't give up no matter how hard it is). **-더라도** is slightly more formal and emphatic than **-아/어도** (even if — simple concession).
**-기로 하다** / **결론짓다** = to decide/conclude: 결론**지었다** (concluded/decided in her mind). **결론짓다** (to draw a conclusion) is a compound of **결론** (conclusion) + **짓다** (to make/form). Compare the more common decision pattern **-기로 하다**: 기차를 타**기로 했다** (decided to take the train). **결론짓다** emphasizes an internal logical conclusion arrived at through reflection, while **-기로 하다** marks a volitional decision about future action.
